Article.ArticleTitle: Loss of ADAM15 prevents necroptosis induction by partial RIPK1 degradation due to enhanced TNF-R1 surface expression and basal caspase-8 activation.

Abstract.AbstractText: Cell death and survival processes must be tightly regulated to ensure proper tissue homeostasis and prevent excessive inflammation and tissue damage. Death receptors, including TNF-R1, can induce either immunogenic (necroptosis) or non-immunogenic (apoptosis) cell death and relay proliferative / cell survival signaling by activating NFκB and MAPK cascades. In a recent report, we identified the metalloproteinase ADAM15 as a possible TNF-responding enzyme, leading to the hypothesis that it regulates either cell survival or death cascades.

CRISPR/Cas-9 was used to knock out the adam15 gene. Loss of gene expression was validated by Western blot and flow cytometry in U937 and Jurkat cells. NFκB, MAPK signaling, and cell death cascades were monitored by Western blot, flow cytometry, and enzyme assays. A bottom-up proteome analysis was performed to elucidate cellular processes affected by ADAM15 loss. The subcellular localization of ADAM15 was monitored by microscopy and immuno-magnetic fractionation.

We identified ADAM15 as a regulator of necroptosis, leaving apoptosis and cell survival signaling unaffected. Loss of ADAM15 resulted in abrogated necroptosis, as evidenced by the application of death ligands TNF, TRAIL, FasL, and TL1a, as well as the BH3 mimetic Obatoclax. We observed enhanced basal Caspase-8 activity, which was not cytotoxic, and partial RIPK1 proteolysis. The loss of ADAM15 was verified in a proteome screen, which revealed alterations in various molecular pathways, including autophagy, organelle trafficking, and sorting. We observed ADAM15 in intracellular compartments, which in part have a lysosomal protein signature. We observed enhanced surface expression of TNF-R1, proposing it as a possible ADAM15 substrate.

ADAM15 is a previously unknown regulator of necroptosis, likely due to its role in modulating intracellular organelle sorting processes. Its proteolytic activity and possible scaffolding capacity for recruiting adaptor molecules make it a veritable drug target. The activation or deactivation of ADAM15 may be exploited to modulate various disease conditions.

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: Department of Infection Prevention and Infectious Diseases, University Hospital of Regensburg, Regensburg, Germany.

Department of Infection Prevention and Infectious Diseases, University Hospital of Regensburg, Regensburg, Germany. Juergen.Fritsch@ukr.de.

Chemical.NameOfSubstance: Receptors, Tumor Necrosis Factor, Type I

MeshHeading.DescriptorName: Receptors, Tumor Necrosis Factor, Type I

OtherAbstract.AbstractText: What we discovered about cell death controlHuman bodies constantly balance cell survival and death to stay healthy. One key player is TNF-R1—a "death receptor" that responds to inflammation signals. When activated, it initiates a chain reaction that can either keep cells alive or trigger their destruction.We focused on a protein called ADAM15, discovered in a previous screening. Usually, cells use two main death pathways:• Apoptosis: An orderly, programmed cell dismantling• Necroptosis: A more explosive, inflammatory deathKey findings:1. When we removed ADAM15 from human cells:○ Necroptosis vanished (even when triggered by multiple signals)○ Apoptosis and survival pathways still worked normally2. ADAM15 affects critical death regulators:○ ADAM15 regulates the presence of TNF-R1 on the cell surface○ RIPK1 (a death pathway "switch") gets partially cut by another protein called caspase-8○ This cutting blocks necroptosis but leaves other functions intact3. ADAM15 also influences:○ Cellular"shipping systems" (organelle movement)○ Protein levels of other death regulatorsWhy it matters:ADAM15 acts as a specialized traffic controller for cell death. In its absence, cells lose their ability to undergo inflammatory necroptosis while maintaining other functions. This reveals a new layer of control in life-or-death cellular decisions, with potential implications for understanding the pathogenesis of inflammatory diseases.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Declarations. Ethics approval and consent to participate: N/A. Competing interests: The authors declare no competing interests.
